title: Laws of Thermochemistry and Enthalpy Change

concepts.title: Enthalpy of Formation
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat or Enthalpy of Formation 

The heat evolved or absorbed when 1 mole of a substance is formed from its elements is called heat of formation. It is denoted by$\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{{\text{f}}^{*}}}}$

For example, heat of formation of carbon dioxide and methane may be expressed as:

$\text{C}(\text{s})+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}(~\text{g})\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f}}}=-74.8~\text{kJ}$

These equations should always be written for one mole as per the definition of the substance to be formed. If for balancing, we require the coefficient $2,.3\ldots $ etc., in the equation then $\Delta {{H}_{f}}$ values should also be multiplied by the same number as discussed earlier.

Standard heat of formation

The heat of formation $\Delta {{H}_{f}}$ depends upon the condition of temperature, pressure and physical state (gas, liquid or solid) of the reactants and the products. Therefore the heat change accompanying the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements when all the substances are in their standard states (1 atm pressure and $298~\text{K}$ ), is called the standard heat of formation. It is expressed as $\Delta {{H}_{{{f}^{*}}}}$

Since no heat changes are involved in the formation of elements form themselves in their standard states, the standard enthalpy of formation of all elements is zero.

For example, the standard enthalpy of formation $\left( \Delta {{\text{H}}_{{{f}^{*}}}} \right)$ for ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{(1)}}$ is $-286~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$ i.e., when one mole of liquid water is formed from its elements ${{\text{H}}_{2(~\text{g})}}$ and ${{\text{O}}_{2(~\text{g})}}$ at $298~\text{K}$ and 1 atm pressure, then $286~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$ of heat is released. The negative value of $\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{{\text{f}}^{\text{. }}}}}$ indicates the formation of a stable compound.

Standard heat of reaction from standard heats of formation 

The knowledge of standard heats of formation of various substances can be used to calculate the heats of reactions under standard conditions. The standard heat of any reaction $(\Delta \text{H})$ is equal to the difference between the $\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f}}}$ of all the reactants i.e.,

$\Delta \text{H}=$ Sum of the standard heats - Sum of the standard heats of formation of products of formation of reactants

$\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\quad \Delta H=\Sigma \Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f (products) }}}-\Sigma \Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f (reactants) }}}$

For a reaction,

$\text{aA}+\text{bB}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{cC}+\text{dD}$

$\Delta \text{H}=\Sigma \Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f (products) }}}-\Sigma \Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f (reactants) }}}$

$=\left[ \text{c}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f}}}(\text{C})+\text{d}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f}}}(\text{D}) \right]-\left[ \text{a}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f}}}(\text{A})+\text{b}\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{f}}}(\text{B}) \right]$

The heat of formation of all elements in their standard state is zero. On this basis, it is evident that the heat of formation of a compound is the heat of the compound.

Elements $\xrightarrow{{}}$ Compound

Heat of formation $={{H}_{f}}($compound$)-{{H}_{f}}$ (elements)

concepts.title: Enthalpy of Combustion
concepts.conceptBody:
Enthalpy of Combustion

The enthalpy of combustion of a compound is the enthalpy change at normal pressure and at constant temperature accompanying complete combustion of one mole of the compound. It is denoted by $\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{c}}}$. Combustion here means the burning of the given compound to the highest oxides of the constituent elements in the presence of excess of oxygen.

For example, the enthalpy of combustion of benzene at $298~\text{K}$ is the enthalpy change of the reaction.

${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{6}}(\text{l})+\frac{15}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}6\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l});\Delta \text{H}=-3268~\text{kJ}$

concepts.title: Enthalpy of Neutralization
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat or Enthalpy of Neutralisation

The reaction in which an acid and a base react to give a salt and water is called neutralization reaction. Neutralization reactions are exothermic in nature. The heat change when one gram equivalent of an acid is completely neutralised by a base or vice versa in dilute solution, is called heat of neutralization.

Examples of heat of neutralization are:

Neutralization of$\text{HCl}$ with $\text{NaOH}$

$\text{HC}{{\text{l}}_{(\text{aq})}}+\text{NaO}{{\text{H}}_{(\text{aq})}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{NaC}{{\text{l}}_{(\text{aq})}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})\Delta \text{H}=-57.1~\text{kJ}$

It is important to note that the term gram equivalent is used in the definition of heat of neutralization. This is because neutralization involves 1 mole of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions and 1 mole of $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions to form 1 mole of water and $57.1~\text{kJ}$ of heat is liberated.

${{\text{H}}^{+}}_{(\text{aq})}+\text{OH}_{(\text{aq})}^{-}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})\,\,\,\,\,\Delta \text{H}=-57.1~\text{kJ}$

Now, one gram equivalent of various acids on complete dissociation liberates one mole of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions. But one mole of the acid may produce more than one mole of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions in solution depending upon its basicity; for example $1~\text{mol}$ of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ gives $2~\text{mol}$ of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions and $1~\text{mol}$ of ${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ gives $3~\text{mol}$ of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions on complete dissociation. But 1 gram equivalent of both $\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right.$ or $\left. {{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)$ produces only $1~\text{mol}$ of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions.

Thus, it is more appropriate to use the term gram equivalent in the definition of enthalpy of neutralization.

The average enthalpy of neutralization of any strong acid by a strong base is found to be -57.7 $\text{kJ}(-13.7\text{kcal})$ irrespective of the nature of acid or the base. This suggests that the net chemical reaction in all neutralization reactions is the same, viz.,

${{\text{H}}^{+}}_{(\text{aq})}+\text{OH}_{(\text{aq})}^{-}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})\Delta \text{H}=-57.1~\text{kJ}$

This is because strong acid and strong base are completely ionized in aqueous solutions. The aqueous solution of one gram equivalent of all strong acids contains the same number of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions. Similarly, aqueous solution of one gram equivalent of all strong bases also contains same number of $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$. The neutralization reactions between strong acids and strong bases in aqueous solutions involve simply the combination of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions (from an acid) and $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions (from a base) to form unionized water molecules.

Neutralization of weak acids and weak bases 

The heat of neutralization of a weak acid or a weak base is less than $-57.1~\text{kJ}$ and is also different for different weak acids or bases.

For example for acetic the enthalpy of neutralization is $-54.9~\text{kJ}$.

concepts.title: Enthalpy of Solution
concepts.conceptBody:
Enthalpy of solution $\left( \mathbf{\Delta }{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{sol }}} \right)$

When a solute is dissolved in a solvent a solution is formed. During dissolution of a solute in any solvent, a certain amount of heat is either absorbed or evolved. Such heat changes under constant pressure conditions are known as the enthalpy of solution. The change in enthalpy when one mole of a solute is dissolved in a specific quantity of a solvent at a given temperature is called enthalpy of solution.

To avoid the amount of solvent, heat of solution is usually defined for an infinite dilute solution. Thus, heat of solution of infinite dilution is the heat change when one mole of a substance is dissolved in such a large quantity of solvent so that further dilution does not give any further heat change.

For example, dissolution of sodium chloride

$NaC{{l}_{(s)}}+aq\quad NaC{{l}_{(aq)}}\quad \Delta H=\Delta {{H}_{sol\text{ }}}=+5.0~kJ$

$\text{Here}'a{q}'$ represents aqueous meaning alarge excess of water.

concepts.title: Enthalpy of Fusion
concepts.conceptBody:
Enthalpy of fusion ($\left. \mathbf{\Delta }{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{fus }}} \right)$

The enthalpy of fusion of a substance is defined as the change in enthalpy when one mole of a solid substance is melted at its melting temperature’. For example, the enthalpy change of the reaction,

$\text{ Ice }(273~\text{K})\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(1)(273~\text{K})\quad \Delta \text{H}=6.0~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$

concepts.title: Enthalpy of Vaporisation
concepts.conceptBody:
Enthalpy of Vaporisation $\left( \mathbf{\Delta }{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{vap}}} \right)$

‘The change in enthalpy when one mole of a liquid is converted into vapours at its boiling temperature is called enthalpy of vaporisation’ $\left( \Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{vap }}} \right)$.

Thus, the enthalpy change of the reaction

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{I}),373~\text{K}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{v}),373~\text{K}\quad \Delta \text{H}=40.7~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$ is the enthalpy of a vaporization of water.

concepts.title: Enthalpy of Sublimation
concepts.conceptBody:
Enthalpy of Sublimation $\left( \mathbf{\Delta }{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{sub}}} \right)$

Sublimation is a process in which a solid substance directly changes into its vapours at any temperature below its melting point. Enthalpy of sublimation is defined as follows:

The change in enthalpy when one mole of a solid substance is converted into its vapours without melting point at a temperature below its melting point is called the enthalpy of sublimation.

For example, when one mole of solid iodine is converted into its vapours at room temperature, heat equal to $62.4~\text{kJ}$ is absorbed. So, the enthalpy of sublimation of iodine is $+62.4~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$, i.e.,

${{\text{I}}_{2}}(~\text{s})\xrightarrow{\Delta }{{\text{I}}_{2}}(\text{v})=62.4~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$

Compounds, which sublime on heating are camphor, dry ice, ammonium chloride etc. The heat of sublimation is related to heat of fusion and heat of vaporization as:

$\Delta {{H}_{sub\lim ation\text{ }}}=\Delta {{H}_{fusion\text{ }}}+\Delta {{H}_{vaporization\text{ }}}$

concepts.title: Hess's law of constant heat summation
concepts.conceptBody:
Hess's law of constant heat summation

This law states that the heat change in a particular reaction is the same whether it takes place in one step or several steps’.

For example, a reactant ‘$\text{A}$’ changes to a product ‘$\text{B}$’ in one step and the heat change during this process is $\Delta H$. If the reaction is carried out in two steps where ‘$A$’ first changes to ‘$C$’ an intermediate stage and then ‘$C$’ changes to ‘$\text{B}$’ in the following step then let the heat change during the formation of ‘$\text{A}$’ to ‘$\text{C}$’ be $\Delta {{\text{H}}_{1}}$ and that from ‘$\text{C}$’ to ‘$\text{B}$’ be $\Delta {{\text{H}}_{2}}$. From Hess’s law the heat change for the reaction is given as:

$\Delta \text{H}=\Delta {{\text{H}}_{1}}+\Delta {{\text{H}}_{2}}$

This means that the amount of heat evolved or absorbed in a chemical reaction depends only upon the energy of initial reactants and the final products. The heat change is independent of the path or the manner in which the change has taken place.

The formation of carbon dioxide from carbon and oxygen can be illustrated as follows. Carbon can be converted into carbon dioxide in two ways. Firstly solid carbon combines with sufficient amount of oxygen to form $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$. The same reaction when carried in presence of lesser amount of oxygen gives carbon monoxide which then gets converted to $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ in step two in the presence of oxygen.

$\text{ Path I : C}(\text{s})+{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\quad \Delta \text{H}=-393.5~\text{kJ}$

$\text{ Path II }:\text{C}(\text{s})+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CO}(\text{g})\quad \Delta {{\text{H}}_{1}}=-110.5~\text{kJ}$

$\text{CO}(\text{g})+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\quad \Delta {{\text{H}}_{2}}=-283.0~\text{kJ}$

$\Delta H=\Delta {{H}_{1}}+\Delta {{H}_{2}}$

Thus, one can conclude that thermochemical equations can be added, subtracted or multiplied like algebraic equations to obtain the desired equation.

concepts.title: Born-Haber cycle
concepts.conceptBody:
Born-Haber Cycle

This cycle is based on thermochemical changes taking place in the formation of a lattice. This cycle can be used to determine lattice energy which cannot be directly measured. It is defined as that energy released when one mole of the atomic compound (lattice) is formed from its isolated ions in the gaseous state under standard condition.

$\text{n}{{\text{A}}^{\text{m}+}}(\text{g})+\text{m}{{\text{B}}^{\text{n}-}}(\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{m}}}(\text{s})$

$\Delta \text{H}=-\text{U (lattice energy)}$

Formation of $\text{NaCl}(\text{s})$ lattice involves thus,

$\text{S}+\text{I}+\frac{\text{D}}{2}-\text{E}-\text{U}=\text{q}$

hence, $\text{U}$ can be calculated.

here, $\text{S}=$ heat of sublimation of $\text{Na}(\text{s})$

$\text{I}=$ ionisation energy of $\text{Na}(\text{g})$

$\text{D}=$ dissociation energy of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

$\text{E}=$ electron affinity of $\text{Cl}(\text{g})$

$\text{U}=$ lattice energy

$q=$ heat of formation of $\text{NaCl}(\text{s})$

If lattice is ${{\operatorname{MgX}}_{2}}(~\text{s})$ then

$\text{S}+\left( {{\text{I}}_{1}}+{{\text{I}}_{2}} \right)+\text{D}-2\text{E}-\text{U}=\text{q}$

where, $\left( {{\text{I}}_{1}}+{{\text{I}}_{2}} \right)=$ total ionisation energy to form $\text{M}{{\text{g}}^{2+}}(\text{g})$.

concepts.title: Bond Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Bond Energy or Bond Enthalpy

In chemical reactions the formation of a chemical bond is accompanied by the release of energy. Conversely energy has to be supplied for the breaking of a bond.

Bond strengths are commonly described by their bond dissociation energy which is the energy required to break one mole of a bond of particular type. This is a definite quantity and is expressed in $\text{kJmo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$. For diatomic molecules the bond dissociation energy is same as bond energy, whereas in polyatomic molecules the bond energy is taken as the mean average of the various bond dissociation energies of the bonds of a given type.

The thermochemical data is useful in determining the bond energies of different bonds.

For example the bond energy of $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond in methane can be calculated form its heat of formation. The heat for formation of methane from carbon and hydrogen has been found to be-1663 $\text{kJmo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$.

$\text{C}(\text{s})+4\text{H}(\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}(~\text{g})\Delta \text{H}=-1663~\text{kJ}$

Methane has four $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds and the energy required to break all the four $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds is 1663 kJ. Therefore the average C-H bond energy is $\frac{1663}{4}=415.75$ or $416~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$.
